evidence_quotes.quote: price presentation/calculation particularly when applied content misleads the consumer.
cancellations of subscriptions, auto-renewals or free trials converted into paid subscriptions.

paragraphs.text: There should be stronger influence on the development of policy and regulatory solutions to improve consumer protection in the area of price presentation/calculation particularly when applied content misleads the consumer. EU consumer in the digital space has to know precisely the price for the goods/services he/she is purchasing. Also, having in mind legal fairness, digital contracts have to be clear for the consumer when they inform about cancellations of subscriptions, auto-renewals or free trials converted into paid subscriptions. It seems that both elements (price presentation + terms of service) constitute backbone of the protection that all consumers in the EU should receive, irrespective the Member State they operate.

evidence_quotes.quote: as long as they are properly structured they can be user-friendly.

paragraphs.text: As for the chabots, given very rapid technological development of these tools, they should be treated with greater attention. They have been introduced to help the consumer to navigate in the digital environment, and as long as they are properly structured they can be user-friendly. It is proposed to take into account this technological aspect into account once structuring corresponding law.

It would be helpful to prepare basic legal rulebook/framework that chatbot should meet particularly from the perspective of consumer protection. Also, the role of the moderator acting on behalf of the digital content provider could be used. For example, the moderator could scrutinize the content displayed on digital platform and assure that the content follows the requirements of the rulebook. Ideally corresponding regulatory body could establish a point of contact with moderators so as to collect their remarks, experience. The communication channel regulatory-vs-moderators would also allow to gather the knowledge over technological developments in the sector so as toadapt corresponding law changes.

Re video games, greater focus should be made on law execution for age verification. Proper implementation of the law and corresponding technical tools could address the concerns for the impact of video games on minors. It seems that recent legal initiative of the European Commission is on the way to respond to this need.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
